paper.title: A single-cell transcriptomic atlas characterizes ageing tissues in the mouse

glossary.Tabula Muris Senis: Single-cell transcriptomic atlas of ageing mouse tissues covering 23 organs across six age groups, also known as the Mouse Ageing Cell Atlas
glossary.Cdkn2a: Cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor 2A gene encoding the senescence marker protein p16
glossary.p16: Protein product of Cdkn2a; a cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor and established cellular senescence marker
glossary.Il1b: Interleukin-1 beta gene encoding a pro-inflammatory cytokine
glossary.Clec4f: C-type lectin domain family 4 member F, a specific marker for liver Kupffer cells
glossary.Kupffer cells: Liver-resident macrophages that play a role in immune defence and tissue homeostasis
glossary.Mesangial cells: Cells in the kidney glomerulus that provide structural support and regulate filtration
glossary.Sirt genes: Sirtuin family genes encoding NAD-dependent deacetylases implicated in delaying cellular senescence
glossary.Atp1a1: Gene encoding the alpha-1 subunit of the sodium/potassium ATPase
glossary.Umod: Gene encoding uromodulin (Tamm-Horsfall protein), the most abundant protein in normal urine
glossary.Jchain: Joining chain gene (also known as Igj), a component of polymeric immunoglobulins and a plasma cell marker
glossary.AP1: Activator protein 1 transcription factor complex including JUN and FOS family members
glossary.Mrc1: Mannose receptor C-type 1 gene involved in immune defence
glossary.ERCC: External RNA Controls Consortium synthetic RNA spike-in controls used to assess technical variation in RNA sequencing
glossary.MHC class I: Major histocompatibility complex class I molecules involved in antigen presentation to cytotoxic T cells
glossary.Fth1: Ferritin heavy chain 1 gene associated with iron storage and neurodegeneration
glossary.Clonal family: Group of B cells sharing similar receptor sequences indicating common clonal origin
glossary.Clonotype: Group of T cells sharing identical or highly similar T cell receptor sequences

nodes.claim_text: The Tabula Muris Senis single-cell transcriptomic atlas reveals that the major hallmarks of ageing -- cellular senescence, tissue composition changes, genomic instability, and immune system remodeling -- are reflected through cell-type-specific molecular changes across a broad range of mouse tissues and cell types.
nodes.evidence_location: Abstract; Main text page 594
nodes.supporting_quote: This transcriptomic atlas--which we denote Tabula Muris Senis, or 'Mouse Ageing Cell Atlas'--provides molecular information about how the most important hallmarks of ageing are reflected in a broad range of tissues and cell types.

nodes.claim_text: Multiple organs undergo tissue-specific age-related changes in cell-type composition and gene expression, with distinct patterns of cell population shifts and compensatory expression changes.
nodes.evidence_location: Abstract; Main text pages 591-593; Fig. 2; Extended Data Figs. 5-6
nodes.supporting_quote: We found cell-specific changes occurring across multiple cell types and organs, as well as age-related changes in the cellular composition of different organs.

nodes.claim_text: Cellular senescence markers, particularly Cdkn2a (p16), show widespread age-related increases in both the fraction of expressing cells and per-cell expression levels across tissues.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text pages 590-591; Fig. 2a-d; Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: The proportion of cells expressing Cdkn2a more than doubled in older mice compared with younger mice according to analysis by both FACS (Fig. 2a) and droplet (Fig. 2b) methods

nodes.claim_text: Somatic mutations accumulate with age across all organs examined, representing a transcriptome-wide hallmark of genomic instability with tissue-specific variation in mutation burden.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Fig. 3; Extended Data Figs. 7-8
nodes.supporting_quote: we observed an age-related increase in the number of mutations across all of the organs we analysed (Fig. 3, Extended Data Fig. 7a, c, e)

nodes.claim_text: The immune system undergoes age-related remodeling including increased lymphocyte clonal expansion and shifts in immune cell transcriptomic diversity and polarization state.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text pages 593-594; Fig. 4; Extended Data Figs. 9-11
nodes.supporting_quote: Ageing also affects the immune system, so we analysed clonal relationships between B cells and T cells throughout the organism.

nodes.claim_text: The bladder undergoes an age-related shift from mesenchymal to urothelial and immune cell composition, accompanied by pro-inflammatory gene expression changes.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Fig. 2e-f; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: The bladder shows pronounced changes in cell-type composition with age (Fig. 2e): whereas the mesenchymal compartment of this tissue decreases by a factor of three over the lifetime of the mouse (Fig. 2e, left), the urothelial compartment increases by a similar amount (Fig. 2e, right).

nodes.claim_text: The kidney shows age-related declines in key functional cell types accompanied by compensatory upregulation of critical genes in remaining cells, suggesting impaired glomerular filtration.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text pages 591-592; Fig. 2g-h; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: Both mesangial cells and capillary endothelial cells are core glomerular cells, and the reduction in their relative abundances with age (Fig. 2g, top)--together with a tissue-wide reduction of Egf and Atp1a1 expression (Fig. 2h, Supplementary Table 6)--suggest an impaired glomerular filtration rate

nodes.claim_text: The spleen undergoes an age-related shift from T cell to B cell and plasma cell dominance.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592; Fig. 2i-j; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: In the spleen, the proportion of T cells decreases with age while the relative amount of plasma cells increases (Fig. 2i).

nodes.claim_text: The liver shows an age-related decrease in hepatocyte proportion with increased immune gene expression and pro-inflammatory changes in Kupffer cells.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Extended Data Fig. 6; Supplementary Tables 5-6
nodes.supporting_quote: We observed an age-related decrease in the relative number of hepatocytes (Extended Data Fig. 6a-d), which is supported by the reduction in the expression of albumin (Alb; Extended Data Fig. 6e, Supplementary Table 6)

nodes.claim_text: The mammary gland shows age-related T cell decline and upregulation of involution-associated transcription factors.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text pages 592-593; Extended Data Fig. 5c-d; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: Similarly, in the mammary gland we observed a decline of the T cell population (Extended Data Fig. 5c).

nodes.claim_text: B cell and T cell clonality increases progressively with age, suggesting diminished capacity to respond to novel pathogens.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Fig. 4a-b; Extended Data Fig. 9
nodes.supporting_quote: These changes in clonality of the T cell repertoire are noteworthy, because they suggest that the immune system of a 24-month-old mouse is less likely to respond to new pathogens.

nodes.claim_text: Immune cells of the brain and kidney undergo significant age-related shifts in transcriptomic diversity and functional polarization.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text pages 593-594; Fig. 4c-f; Extended Data Figs. 10-11
nodes.supporting_quote: We observed significant changes in diversity for cells of the immune system that originate from the brain and the kidney (Fig. 4c, Extended Data Fig. 11a, b).

nodes.claim_text: Brain microglia transition through intermediate transcriptomic states to acquire a pro-inflammatory gene expression signature in old age, including upregulation of MHC class I and interferon-responsive genes.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 594; Fig. 4d-e; Extended Data Fig. 11e; Supplementary Table 10
nodes.supporting_quote: In brain myeloid microglial cells, the majority of young (3 month) microglia occupy clusters 1 and 6, whereas old (18 month and 24 month) microglia constitute the vast majority of cells in clusters 10, 12 and 14 (Fig. 4d).

nodes.claim_text: Kidney macrophages segregate into age-dependent populations with distinct anti-inflammatory and pro-inflammatory gene signatures.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 594; Fig. 4f; Extended Data Fig. 11f; Supplementary Table 10
nodes.supporting_quote: Regarding kidney macrophages, we found two clusters for which the composition changed markedly with age.

nodes.claim_text: Age-related changes in bulk gene expression across tissues are attributable to both changes in the numbers of cells of each type in a population and changes in per-cell gene expression levels.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from 23 tissues and organs of male and female C57BL/6JN mice at six age groups (1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months) using FACS/Smart-seq2 (110,824 annotated cells) and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium (245,389 annotated cells) platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: comparison of bulk and single-cell gene expression changes with age
nodes.analysis_detail: For each tissue, single-cell data from male mice was used with log(1+cpm) transformed count matrices. Two groups were defined: young (age <= 3 months) and old (age > 3 months). For each gene, the log2 fold-change of cell count (fraction of cells expressing the gene) and read count (mean expression in expressing cells) was computed between old and young. Bulk RNA-seq data was analyzed using DESeq2, and Spearman correlation of normalized expression with age was computed. Genes were classified as bulk-increasing (rho > 0.7) or bulk-decreasing (rho < -0.7). Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney test was used to compare single-cell log2 fold-changes between bulk-defined groups, using the U statistic for effect size.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 590; Extended Data Fig. 5a-b
nodes.supporting_quote: we observed that-in many cases-changes in gene expression are due to both changes in the numbers of cells in a population and changes in the gene expression levels in each cell

nodes.claim_text: The proportion of cells expressing the senescence marker Cdkn2a more than doubled in older mice compared with younger mice, confirmed by both FACS and droplet single-cell sequencing platforms.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from 23 tissues and organs of C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using both FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: cell fraction analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The fraction of cells expressing Cdkn2a (>0 counts) was computed for each age group across all tissues for both FACS and droplet platforms. Data were log-transformed and scaled. P values were obtained using Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon rank-sum two-sided test with individual data points. n = 44,518, 34,027 and 31,551 individual cells for FACS at 3, 18 and 24 months; n = 25,980, 45,602, 44,645, 35,828, 37,660 and 55,674 individual cells for droplet at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24 and 30 months.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 590-591; Fig. 2a-b
nodes.supporting_quote: The proportion of cells expressing Cdkn2a more than doubled in older mice compared with younger mice according to analysis by both FACS (Fig. 2a) and droplet (Fig. 2b) methods

nodes.claim_text: Among previously characterized senescence markers, Cdkn2a shows the highest correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells in which it is expressed.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from 23 tissues and organs of C57BL/6JN mice across six age groups using both FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: correlation analysis of senescence markers with age
nodes.analysis_detail: A list of previously characterized senescence markers was compiled from the literature. For each marker gene, the fraction of cells expressing that gene was computed across all age groups and tissues. The correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells expressing each marker was computed. Markers were ranked by correlation strength to identify which had the strongest age association.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: Cdkn2a has the highest correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells in which it is expressed

nodes.claim_text: The bladder undergoes pronounced age-related compositional changes: the mesenchymal compartment decreases by approximately a factor of three over the mouse lifespan while the urothelial compartment increases by a similar amount.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on bladder tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform. n = 970, 3,804, 2,739 and 3,864 individual cells for bladder at 1, 3, 18 and 24 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The relative proportion of each cell type in the bladder was computed per age group. Cell types were assigned through clustering and annotation. Proportions of the mesenchymal and urothelial compartments were regressed against age. Changes were considered significant with P < 0.05 and r^2 > 0.5 using a two-sided Wald test with t-distribution of the test statistic.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Fig. 2e
nodes.supporting_quote: The bladder shows pronounced changes in cell-type composition with age (Fig. 2e): whereas the mesenchymal compartment of this tissue decreases by a factor of three over the lifetime of the mouse (Fig. 2e, left), the urothelial compartment increases by a similar amount (Fig. 2e, right).

nodes.claim_text: The relative abundance of mesangial cells, capillary endothelial cells, loop of Henle ascending limb epithelial cells, and loop of Henle thick ascending limb epithelial cells decreases with age in the kidney.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform. n = 2,488, 2,832, 3,806, 2,257, 6,384 and 5,713 individual cells for kidney at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24 and 30 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The relative proportion of each cell type in the kidney was computed per age group. Proportions of mesangial cells, capillary endothelial cells, loop of Henle ascending limb epithelial cells, and loop of Henle thick ascending limb epithelial cells were regressed against age. Changes were considered significant with P < 0.05 and r^2 > 0.5 using a two-sided Wald test.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591-592; Fig. 2g
nodes.supporting_quote: Age-related changes in the kidney include a decrease in the relative abundance of mesangial cells, capillary endothelial cells, loop of Henle ascending limb epithelial cells and loop of Henle thick ascending limb epithelial cells (Fig. 2g).

nodes.claim_text: The proportion of T cells in the spleen decreases with age while the relative amount of plasma cells increases.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on spleen tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform. n = 8,839, 7,141, 6,395, 5,245 and 8,946 individual cells for spleen at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24 and 30 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The relative proportion of each cell type in the spleen was computed per age group. T cell and plasma cell proportions were regressed against age. Changes were considered significant with P < 0.05 and r^2 > 0.5 using a two-sided Wald test with t-distribution of the test statistic.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592; Fig. 2i-j
nodes.supporting_quote: In the spleen, the proportion of T cells decreases with age while the relative amount of plasma cells increases (Fig. 2i).

nodes.claim_text: The pro-inflammatory cytokine gene Il1b shows markedly increased expression with age in the liver, with the number of cells co-expressing Il1b and the Kupffer cell marker Clec4f increasing with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on liver tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: co-expression analysis of Il1b and Clec4f across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: Cells co-expressing Il1b and the Kupffer cell marker Clec4f were identified in the liver single-cell dataset. The number of cells positive for both markers was quantified across age groups (1, 3, 18, 21, 24, 30 months). Il1b expression levels and the fraction of Kupffer cells expressing Il1b were assessed for age-related changes.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Extended Data Fig. 6f-j
nodes.supporting_quote: when co-staining with Il1b, we found that the number of cells expressing both Clec4f and Il1b increased with age (Extended Data Fig. 6h-j).

nodes.claim_text: The number of somatic mutations increases with age across all organs examined, with tongue and bladder being the most affected tissues.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA sequencing was performed using FACS/Smart-seq2 on cells from 23 tissues and organs of C57BL/6JN mice at ages 3, 18, and 24 months. Smart-seq2 data was used because it provides full-length transcript coverage enabling mutation detection.
nodes.analysis_type: somatic mutation detection from single-cell transcriptomes
nodes.analysis_detail: Single-nucleotide polymorphisms were identified across all FACS samples using the Genome Analysis Toolkit (GATK) HaplotypeCaller. For each tissue, genes expressed in at least 75% of cells for each age group were selected. Genes mutated in more than 60% of cells were removed to eliminate germline bias. The average number of mutations in the gene set per cell was computed per age group. Sequencing coverage and gene expression levels were controlled for. Results were compared across tissues to identify the most affected organs.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Fig. 3a-f; Extended Data Fig. 7
nodes.supporting_quote: we focused on genes that were expressed in at least 75% of cells for each age group within a particular tissue, and observed an age-related increase in the number of mutations across all of the organs we analysed (Fig. 3, Extended Data Fig. 7a, c, e)-tongue and bladder were the most affected

nodes.claim_text: The proportion of B cells belonging to a clonal family increases progressively with age, from ~9% at 3 months to ~20% at 18 months and ~38% at 24 months.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA sequencing was performed using FACS/Smart-seq2. B cell receptors were assembled from 6,050 cells with assembled BCR sequences across three age groups (3, 18, and 24 months).
nodes.analysis_type: B cell receptor clonotype analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: B cell receptor sequences were computationally reconstructed from FACS single-cell data using singlecell-ige with default configuration settings. Species was set to mouse. Clonal families were defined based on shared receptor sequences. The proportion of B cells belonging to a clonal family was computed per age group. n = 1,818 cells at 3 months, 1,356 at 18 months, and 2,876 at 24 months.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Fig. 4a; Extended Data Fig. 9a
nodes.supporting_quote: whereas most of the cells at 3 months were not part of a clone (9% were part of a clonal family), the number of B cells belonging to a clonotype doubled at 18 months (20%) when compared to 3 months, and doubled again from 18 months to 24 months (around 38%).

nodes.claim_text: The proportion of T cells that are part of a clone increases from ~3% at 3 months to ~23% at 18 months and ~20% at 24 months, indicating increased clonality in the T cell repertoire with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA sequencing was performed using FACS/Smart-seq2. T cell receptors were assembled from 6,000 cells with assembled TCR sequences across three age groups (3, 18, and 24 months).
nodes.analysis_type: T cell receptor clonotype analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: T cell receptor sequences were computationally reconstructed from FACS single-cell data using TraCeR v0.5 with dependencies igblast v.0.43.1, Salmon v.0.8.2, Trinity v.2.4.0, and GRCm38 reference genome. Clonotype assignment was performed using the tracer summarize function with species set to Mmus. The proportion of T cells that are part of a clone was computed per age group. n = 2,076 cells at 3 months, 2,056 at 18 months, and 1,868 at 24 months.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Fig. 4b; Extended Data Fig. 9b
nodes.supporting_quote: only around 3% (55 out of 1,895) of the cells at 3 months were part of a clone. At 18 months and 24 months, this value increased to around 23% (479 out of 2,056) and around 20% (348 out of 1,780) of the cells, respectively

nodes.claim_text: Among all cell types and tissues examined, immune cells from the brain (myeloid microglial cells) and the kidney (macrophages) show significant age-related changes in transcriptomic diversity.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on brain myeloid and kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice across multiple age groups using both FACS and droplet platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: transcriptomic diversity analysis across cell types and tissues
nodes.analysis_detail: For each tissue-cell type combination per mouse, cells were clustered using six configurations: resolution parameters (0.3, 0.5, 0.7) x clustering method (Louvain, Leiden). The Shannon entropy of cluster assignment was computed as a diversity score. The diversity score was regressed against age to detect systematic age-related changes. FDR correction was applied for multiple comparisons. A tissue-cell type was selected if the slope had consistent sign in all six clustering configurations and at least two out of six had FDR < 0.3.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593-594; Fig. 4c; Extended Data Figs. 10-11a-b
nodes.supporting_quote: We observed significant changes in diversity for cells of the immune system that originate from the brain and the kidney (Fig. 4c, Extended Data Fig. 11a, b).

nodes.claim_text: Young microglia transition through intermediate transcriptomic states before acquiring the gene expression signature characteristic of old microglia.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on brain myeloid microglial cells using FACS/Smart-seq2 from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 18, and 24 months. n = 4,532, 4,461 and 4,424 individual microglia at 3, 18, and 24 months.
nodes.analysis_type: trajectory analysis of microglial cells
nodes.analysis_detail: Partition-based graph abstraction (PAGA) was used to reconstruct the ageing trajectory in brain myeloid microglial cells from FACS data. Cells from 3, 18, and 24 months were clustered using Leiden algorithm. The age composition of each cluster was assessed. Young (3-month) microglia predominantly occupied clusters 1 and 6, 18-month cells occupied intermediate clusters, and old (24-month) microglia occupied clusters 10, 12, and 14, revealing a trajectory from young to old transcriptomic states.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 594; Fig. 4d; Extended Data Fig. 11e
nodes.supporting_quote: Trajectory analysis suggests that young microglia go through an intermediate state, represented by the clusters mostly occupied by 18-month microglial cells, before acquiring the signature of old microglia (Extended Data Fig. 11e).

nodes.claim_text: Old (18- and 24-month) microglia upregulate MHC class I genes (H2-D1, H2-K1, B2m) and genes associated with degenerative disease (such as Fth1) compared to young (3-month) microglia.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on brain myeloid microglial cells using FACS/Smart-seq2 from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 18, and 24 months.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression between age-defined microglial clusters
nodes.analysis_detail: Differential gene expression analysis was performed between young-enriched (clusters 1 and 6) and old-enriched (clusters 10, 12, and 14) brain myeloid microglial cell populations from FACS data. Genes significantly upregulated in old microglial clusters were identified, including MHC class I genes (H2-D1, H2-K1, B2m) and disease-associated genes (Fth1).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 594; Fig. 4d-e; Supplementary Table 10
nodes.supporting_quote: These cells upregulate major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I genes (H2-D1, H2-K1 and B2m), along with genes associated with degenerative disease (for example, Fth1).

nodes.claim_text: Kidney macrophages segregate into two transcriptomically distinct populations with age-dependent composition: one enriched for anti-inflammatory genes and predominantly composed of young cells, and another enriched for pro-inflammatory genes and predominantly composed of old cells.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform. n = 62, 139, 264, 105, 284 and 553 individual macrophage cells at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months.
nodes.analysis_type: clustering and age-composition analysis of kidney macrophages
nodes.analysis_detail: Kidney macrophages from the droplet dataset were clustered using Leiden algorithm. Two clusters with markedly age-dependent composition were identified: cluster 10 (primarily composed of cells from 1- and 3-month-old mice) and cluster 13 (primarily composed of cells from 18-, 21-, 24-, and 30-month-old mice). The age composition of each cluster was quantified to confirm the age-dependent segregation.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 594; Fig. 4f; Extended Data Fig. 11f
nodes.supporting_quote: Regarding kidney macrophages, we found two clusters for which the composition changed markedly with age. Cluster 10 is primarily composed of cells from 1-month-old and 3-month-old mice, whereas cluster 13 is mostly composed of cells from 18-month-old, 21-month-old, 24-month-old and 30-month-old mice (Fig. 4f).

nodes.claim_text: The expression level of Cdkn2a (p16) in cells that express it approximately doubled with age.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from 23 tissues and organs of C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using both FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: per-cell expression level analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The median expression of Cdkn2a was computed specifically in cells that express it (>0 counts) for each age group using both FACS and droplet platforms. Expression values were log-transformed and scaled. P values were obtained using Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon rank-sum two-sided test.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 590-591; Fig. 2c-d
nodes.supporting_quote: this was accompanied by a twofold increase in the expression levels of p16 in cells in which it was expressed (Fig. 2c, d).

nodes.claim_text: In 30-month-old mice, the fraction of cells expressing Cdkn2a (p16) was smaller than in 24-month-old mice.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from multiple tissues of C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: cell fraction analysis comparing 30-month and 24-month age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The fraction of cells expressing Cdkn2a (>0 counts) was computed per age group for the droplet platform, covering ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months. The fraction at 30 months was compared to the fraction at 24 months, revealing a decrease at the oldest time point.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Fig. 2b
nodes.supporting_quote: Notably, in 30-month-old mice the fraction of cells that expressed p16 was smaller than in 24-month-old mice, perhaps because long-living animals have a slower rate of senescence.

nodes.claim_text: Among senescence-associated genes, E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf, and Itgax show positive correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells expressing them.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from 23 tissues and organs of C57BL/6JN mice across six age groups using both FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: correlation analysis of senescence markers with age
nodes.analysis_detail: A list of previously characterized senescence markers was compiled. For each marker gene, the fraction of cells expressing that gene was computed across all age groups and tissues. The correlation between ageing and the fraction of expressing cells was computed. Genes with positive correlation were identified, including E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf, and Itgax.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: other genes with positive correlation include E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf and Itgax

nodes.claim_text: The fraction of cells expressing Sirt family genes (Sirt3, Sirt4, and Sirt5) decreases with age.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from 23 tissues and organs of C57BL/6JN mice across six age groups using both FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: cell fraction analysis of sirtuin genes across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: For Sirt family genes (Sirt3, Sirt4, Sirt5), the fraction of cells expressing each gene (>0 counts) was computed across all age groups and tissues. An age-related decrease in the fraction of expressing cells was observed for these genes.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Supplementary Table 4
nodes.supporting_quote: For some genes, including members of the Sirt family (Sirt3, Sirt4 and Sirt5), the fraction of cells in which they were expressed was found to decrease with age

nodes.claim_text: Differential gene expression in the bladder shows stromal-associated genes (such as Col1a1, Col1a2, Col3a1, and Dcn) downregulated with age while epithelial-associated genes (such as Krt15, Krt18, and Sfn) are upregulated.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on bladder tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Differential gene expression was performed on bladder tissue using MAST, treating age as a continuous covariate while controlling for sex and technology. Genes were classified as significant under a false-discovery rate (FDR) threshold of 0.01 and an age coefficient threshold of 0.005 (corresponding to approximately 10% fold change). Stromal-associated genes (Col1a1, Col1a2, Col3a1, Dcn) and epithelial-associated genes (Krt15, Krt18, Sfn) were examined for age-related expression changes.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Fig. 2f
nodes.supporting_quote: we found that stromal-associated genes (Col1a1, Col1a2, Col3a1 and Dcn) are downregulated while epithelial-associated genes (Krt15, Krt18 and Sfn) are upregulated

nodes.claim_text: The endothelial population in the bladder declines with age while the leukocyte population increases, accompanied by tissue-wide overexpression of immune-related genes including Lgals3, Igfbp2, and Ly6d.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on bladder tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion and differential gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Cell type proportions were computed per age group in the bladder. Tissue-wide differential gene expression was performed using MAST with age as a continuous covariate, controlling for sex and technology. FDR threshold of 0.01 and age coefficient threshold of 0.005 were applied. Immune-related genes Lgals3, Igfbp2, and Ly6d were identified as significantly overexpressed with age.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Fig. 2f; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: The increase in the leukocyte population could indicate an inflammatory tissue microenvironment, a common hallmark of ageing that is consistent with literature on overactive bladders and is supported by a significant overexpression of Lgals3, Igfbp2 and Ly6d across the tissue

nodes.claim_text: Old bladder leukocytes show increased expression of pro-inflammatory markers (including Cd14, Lgals3, and Tnfrsf12a) and decreased expression of anti-inflammatory markers (including Cd9 and Cd81).
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on bladder tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression in bladder leukocyte subpopulation
nodes.analysis_detail: Gene expression was compared between old and young leukocytes in the bladder using differential expression analysis. Pro-inflammatory markers (Cd14, Lgals3, Tnfrsf12a) and anti-inflammatory markers (Cd9, Cd81) were assessed for age-related changes within the leukocyte compartment.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: we observed that old leukocytes showed increased expression of pro-inflammatory markers-such as Cd14, Lgals3 and Tnfrsf12a-and decreased expression of anti-inflammatory markers such as Cd9 and Cd81

nodes.claim_text: Tissue-wide expression of Egf and Atp1a1 in the kidney decreases with age, suggesting impaired glomerular filtration.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: tissue-wide differential gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Tissue-wide differential gene expression was performed in kidney using MAST with age as a continuous covariate, controlling for sex and technology. FDR threshold of 0.01 and age coefficient threshold of 0.005 were applied. Egf and Atp1a1 expression changes across the entire kidney tissue were assessed for age-related trends.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591-592; Fig. 2h; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: together with a tissue-wide reduction of Egf and Atp1a1 expression (Fig. 2h, Supplementary Table 6)-suggest an impaired glomerular filtration rate

nodes.claim_text: Local Atp1a1 expression increases with age in both capillary endothelial cells and mesangial cells in the kidney, suggesting a compensatory mechanism counteracting the declining proportion of these cell types.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: cell-type-specific gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Atp1a1 expression was analyzed within specific cell types (capillary endothelial cells and mesangial cells) in the kidney across age groups. Cell-type-specific differential expression was performed to identify whether local expression increased with age within these declining cell types.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591-592; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: Notably, local Atp1a1 expression increases with age in both capillary endothelial cells and mesangial cells, suggesting that a compensation mechanism counteracts the effects of the declining proportion of these cells with age.

nodes.claim_text: Expression of Umod (encoding uromodulin) decreases across the kidney tissue with age due to the reduced proportion of ascending limb epithelial cells that produce it.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: tissue-wide gene expression analysis linked to cell type composition
nodes.analysis_detail: Tissue-wide Umod expression was assessed across age groups using differential gene expression with MAST. The declining proportion of ascending limb epithelial cells (the primary Umod-expressing cell type) was correlated with the tissue-wide reduction in Umod expression.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592; Fig. 2g; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: the expression of Umod-which encodes uromodulin, the most abundant protein in urine-is also reduced across the tissue

nodes.claim_text: Umod expression increases within the ascending limb epithelial cell types that become less abundant with age, paralleling the compensatory pattern observed for Atp1a1.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: cell-type-specific gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Umod expression was analyzed specifically within the ascending limb epithelial cell types that become less abundant with age. Cell-type-specific expression was compared across age groups to determine whether local expression compensated for declining cell proportions, paralleling the pattern observed for Atp1a1.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: As with Atp1a1, we see that the expression of Umod increases in a cell type that becomes less abundant with age, leading to an overall reduction of its expression in the organ.

nodes.claim_text: Age-related changes in spleen composition are supported by upregulation of B cell and plasma cell markers Cd79a and Jchain and downregulation of the T cell marker Cd3d.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on spleen tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Tissue-wide differential gene expression was performed in spleen using MAST with age as a continuous covariate, controlling for sex and technology. FDR threshold of 0.01 and age coefficient threshold of 0.005 were applied. B cell/plasma cell markers (Cd79a, Jchain) and T cell marker (Cd3d) were examined for age-related expression changes.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592; Fig. 2j; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: This is supported by the upregulation of B cell and plasma cell marker genes (Cd79a and Jchain (also known as Igj), respectively; Fig. 2j, Supplementary Table 6) and the downregulation of Cd3d

nodes.claim_text: The T cell population in the mammary gland declines with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on mammary gland tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 18, and 21 months using the FACS/Smart-seq2 platform. n = 6,393, 3,635 and 5,549 individual cells at 3, 18, and 21 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The relative proportion of T cells in the mammary gland was computed per age group. Changes were assessed using a two-sided Wald test with P < 0.05 and r^2 > 0.7 for a hypothesis test that the slope is zero.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592; Extended Data Fig. 5c
nodes.supporting_quote: in the mammary gland we observed a decline of the T cell population

nodes.claim_text: AP1 transcription factors (Junb, Jund, and Fos) are upregulated with age in the mammary gland, consistent with normal mammary gland involution.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on mammary gland tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 18, and 21 months using the FACS/Smart-seq2 platform.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Differential gene expression was performed on mammary gland tissue using MAST with age as a continuous covariate, controlling for sex and technology. FDR threshold of 0.01 and age coefficient threshold of 0.005 were applied. AP1 transcription factor genes (Junb, Jund, Fos) were identified among genes upregulated with age.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592-593; Extended Data Fig. 5d; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: genes encoding AP1 transcription factors (Junb, Jund and Fos) were upregulated with age (Extended Data Fig. 5d, Supplementary Table 6), consistent with the observation that normal involution of the mammary gland is accompanied by increased expression of this gene family

nodes.claim_text: The relative proportion of hepatocytes in the liver decreases with age, supported by a tissue-wide reduction in albumin (Alb) expression.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on liver tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using both FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platforms. n = 2,791, 2,832, 3,806, 2,257, 6,384 and 5,713 individual cells for liver at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24 and 30 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion and tissue-wide gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: The relative proportion of hepatocytes in the liver was computed per age group. Changes were assessed using a two-sided Wald test with P < 0.05 and r^2 > 0.7. Tissue-wide expression of albumin (Alb) was assessed using differential gene expression with MAST to corroborate the compositional change.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Extended Data Fig. 6a, 6e; Supplementary Table 5
nodes.supporting_quote: We observed an age-related decrease in the relative number of hepatocytes (Extended Data Fig. 6a-d), which is supported by the reduction in the expression of albumin (Alb; Extended Data Fig. 6e, Supplementary Table 6)

nodes.claim_text: Differential gene expression in the liver reveals an increased immune signature with age, illustrated by overexpression of H2-Aa, H2-Ab1, H2-D1, H2-Eb1, Cd74, and Lyz2.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on liver tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using both FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Differential gene expression was performed on liver tissue using MAST with age as a continuous covariate, controlling for sex and technology. FDR threshold of 0.01 and age coefficient threshold of 0.005 (corresponding to approximately 10% fold change) were applied. Immune-related genes (H2-Aa, H2-Ab1, H2-D1, H2-Eb1, Cd74, Lyz2) were identified among the top upregulated genes with age.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Extended Data Fig. 6e
nodes.supporting_quote: Differential gene expression analysis revealed an increased immune signature, as illustrated by the overexpression of H2-Aa, H2-Ab1, H2-D1, H2-Eb1, Cd74, Lyz2 and others (Extended Data Fig. 6e).

nodes.claim_text: Mrc1 expression increases in liver Kupffer cells with age, whereas overall Mrc1 expression in liver endothelial cells decreases with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on liver tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at multiple age groups using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: cell-type-specific differential gene expression analysis
nodes.analysis_detail: Mrc1 expression was analyzed separately in Kupffer cells and endothelial cells in the liver using cell-type-specific differential expression tables. Age-related changes in Mrc1 expression were assessed independently within each cell type, revealing opposite directional changes.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Extended Data Fig. 6k; Supplementary Table 6
nodes.supporting_quote: We identified increased expression of Mrc1 with age in Kupffer cells, whereas the overall Mrc1 in endothelial cells decreased with age (Supplementary Table 6).

nodes.claim_text: The age-related increase in somatic mutations exceeds what is expected from amplification and sequencing errors, as verified using ERCC spike-in controls that show no corresponding age-related increase.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA sequencing was performed using FACS/Smart-seq2 with ERCC spike-in controls spiked into each well. Smart-seq2 data was used because ERCC controls were included in the FACS protocol.
nodes.analysis_type: technical control validation of mutation detection
nodes.analysis_detail: ERCC spike-in controls spiked into each well were analyzed for mutations using the same GATK pipeline as endogenous genes. The mean number of mutations in ERCC spike-ins per cell was compared between age groups. Sequencing coverage and gene expression levels were additionally controlled for. The number of mutations in endogenous genes exceeded those in ERCC controls, and ERCC controls showed no age-related increase, confirming that the observed mutation accumulation is biological rather than technical.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; Fig. 3; Extended Data Fig. 8
nodes.supporting_quote: We controlled for sequencing coverage and gene expression levels (Extended Data Fig. 8a, c, e), and verified that the number of mutations exceeded those expected from amplification and sequencing errors, which can be estimated using External RNA Controls Consortium (ERCC) controls that were spiked into each well

nodes.claim_text: Old microglia are enriched for interferon responsive and regulatory genes (including Oasl2, Ifit3, Stat1, and Irf7) compared to young microglia, suggesting expansion of a pro-inflammatory microglial subset in the ageing brain.
nodes.experiment_detail: Full-length transcript single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on brain myeloid microglial cells using FACS/Smart-seq2 from C57BL/6JN mice at 3, 18, and 24 months.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression analysis of microglial clusters
nodes.analysis_detail: Gene expression in old-enriched microglial clusters (10, 12, 14) was compared against young-enriched clusters (1, 6) in brain myeloid cells from FACS data. Enrichment for interferon responsive or regulatory genes (Oasl2, Oas1a, Ifit3, Rtp4, Bst2, Stat1, Irf7, Ifitm3, Usp18, Ifi204, Ifit2) was identified in the old clusters, indicating expansion of a pro-inflammatory microglial subset.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 594; Supplementary Table 10
nodes.supporting_quote: gene expression in clusters 10, 12 and 14 is enriched with interferon responsive or regulatory genes (for example, Oasl2, Oas1a, Ifit3, Rtp4, Bst2, Stat1, Irf7, Ifitm3, Usp18, Ifi204 and Ifit2), suggesting an expansion of this small pro-inflammatory subset of microglia in the ageing brain

nodes.claim_text: The kidney macrophage anti-inflammatory population is enriched for genes such as Il10, H2-Eb1, H2-Ab1, Cd74, and Cxcl16, while the pro-inflammatory population is enriched for genes such as Hp, Itgal, Msrb1, and Gngt2.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium platform.
nodes.analysis_type: differential gene expression between kidney macrophage clusters
nodes.analysis_detail: Differential gene expression was performed between cluster 10 (young-enriched, anti-inflammatory) and cluster 13 (old-enriched, pro-inflammatory) kidney macrophages. Anti-inflammatory gene enrichment (Il10, H2-Eb1, H2-Ab1, H2-Aq, Cd74, Cxcl16, Hexb, Cd81, C1qb, Cd72) was identified in cluster 10, and pro-inflammatory gene enrichment (Hp, Itgal, Msrb1, Gngt2) was identified in cluster 13.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 594; Extended Data Fig. 11f; Supplementary Table 10
nodes.supporting_quote: Differential gene expression analysis revealed that cluster 10 is enriched for an M2-macrophage gene signature (for example, Il10, H2-Eb1, H2-Ab1, H2-Aq, Cd74, Cxcl16, Hexb, Cd81, C1qb and Cd72) whereas cluster 13 resembles a M1-proinflammatory macrophage state (for example, Hp, Itgal, Msrb1 and Gngt2)

nodes.claim_text: The relative abundance of precursor B cells in the bone marrow decreases with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on bone marrow tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 1, 3, 18, 21, 24, and 30 months using the FACS/Smart-seq2 platform. n = 3,027, 8,559, 11,496, 5,216, 12,943 and 13,496 individual cells at 1, 3, 18, 21, 24 and 30 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The relative proportion of precursor B cells in the bone marrow was computed per age group. Changes were assessed using a two-sided Wald test with P < 0.05 and r^2 > 0.5 for a hypothesis test that the slope is zero.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Fig. 5e; Supplementary Table 5
nodes.supporting_quote: Relative abundances of marrow precursor B cells change significantly with age

nodes.claim_text: Cells annotated as B cells or endothelial cells from different organs cluster together by whole-transcriptome analysis irrespective of tissue of origin or sequencing method, validating the consistency of cell-type annotations across the atlas.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on cells from 23 tissues and organs of C57BL/6JN mice using both FACS/Smart-seq2 (110,824 annotated cells) and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium (245,389 annotated cells) platforms. n = 356,213 individual cells combined.
nodes.analysis_type: cross-tissue cell type clustering validation
nodes.analysis_detail: B cells and endothelial cells were independently annotated for each organ and then clustered together by unbiased whole-transcriptome Louvain clustering, irrespective of the organ from which they originated or the sequencing technology used. Cluster co-occupancy was assessed to verify that cells of the same type from different tissues and platforms grouped together, validating annotation consistency across the atlas.
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 590; Fig. 1e-f; Extended Data Fig. 1g-l
nodes.supporting_quote: cells annotated as B cells or endothelial cells tend to occupy the same clusters irrespective of their tissue of origin or the method with which they were processed (Fig. 1e, f, Extended Data Fig. 1g-l).

nodes.claim_text: The relative abundance of skin keratinocyte stem cells changes significantly with age.
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on skin tissue from C57BL/6JN mice at ages 3, 18, 21, and 24 months using the FACS/Smart-seq2 platform. n = 2,346, 1,494, 4,352 and 1,122 individual cells at 3, 18, 21 and 24 months.
nodes.analysis_type: cell type proportion analysis across age groups
nodes.analysis_detail: The relative proportion of keratinocyte stem cells in the skin was computed per age group. Changes were assessed using a two-sided Wald test with P < 0.05 and r^2 > 0.7 for a hypothesis test that the slope is zero.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Fig. 5g; Supplementary Table 5
nodes.supporting_quote: Relative abundances of skin keratinocyte stem cells change significantly with age

nodes.claim_text: The diversity score metric for identifying age-susceptible cell types is not confounded by the number of genes expressed per cell.
nodes.experiment_type: single-cell RNA sequencing (FACS/Smart-seq2 and microfluidic droplet/10x Chromium)
nodes.experiment_detail: Single-cell RNA sequencing was performed on brain myeloid and kidney tissue from C57BL/6JN mice across multiple age groups using both FACS and droplet platforms.
nodes.analysis_type: confounding analysis for transcriptomic diversity metric
nodes.analysis_detail: The relationship between the diversity score (Shannon entropy of cluster assignment) and the number of genes expressed per cell was assessed across age groups. The analysis confirmed that age-related changes in the diversity score for brain myeloid microglial cells and kidney macrophages are not artifacts of varying gene detection sensitivity across age groups.
nodes.evidence_location: Extended Data Fig. 11c-d
nodes.supporting_quote: These results were not confounded by the number of genes expressed per cell (Extended Data Fig. 11c, d).

nodes.claim_text: Sirtuin proteins encoded by Sirt family genes are essential in delaying cellular senescence (Saunders & Verdin 2007; Lee et al. 2019).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 591; citing literature on sirtuins and cellular senescence
nodes.supporting_quote: this is consistent with previous literature finding that sirtuins--the protein products of Sirt genes--are essential in delaying cellular senescence

nodes.claim_text: Pro-inflammatory macrophages have been shown to drive cellular senescence, with Il1b identified as showing markedly age-dependent expression in the liver (prior literature).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 593; citing literature on macrophages and senescence
nodes.supporting_quote: Previous findings suggested that pro-inflammatory macrophages drive cellular senescence, and identified the gene Il1b as showing markedly different expression in the liver with age

nodes.claim_text: Age-related decline of T cell populations is associated with increased risk of infectious disease and cancer (prior literature).
nodes.evidence_location: Main text page 592; citing literature on T cell decline and disease
nodes.supporting_quote: Age-related decline of T cell populations has been associated with increased risk of infectious disease and cancer

excluded_borderline_claims.claim_text: Approximately 55 of the top 200 differentially expressed genes between young and old microglial clusters overlap with a previously reported Alzheimer's disease-specific microglial gene signature.
excluded_borderline_claims.reason: Requires an external gene list (the Alzheimer's disease-specific microglial signature from Keren-Shaul et al.) not among the paper's own datasets. The differential expression itself is derivable, but the overlap comparison depends on an external classification.

excluded_borderline_claims.claim_text: Hepatocyte cell size increases and hepatocyte cell number decreases with age in the liver.
excluded_borderline_claims.reason: This finding is from bright-field imaging experiments (Extended Data Fig. 6b-d), not from the scRNA-seq datasets described in the dataset profile.

excluded_borderline_claims.claim_text: The number of Mrc1-expressing liver sinusoidal endothelial cells (LSECs) increases with age, although Mrc1 expression per LSEC does not increase.
excluded_borderline_claims.reason: Demonstrated primarily by in situ RNA staining (Extended Data Fig. 6l-o), not directly derivable from the scRNA-seq dataset alone.

excluded_borderline_claims.claim_text: The number of Clec4f-positive Kupffer cells does not change significantly with age in the liver.
excluded_borderline_claims.reason: Quantified by in situ RNA staining (Extended Data Fig. 6g-h); the primary evidence is from staining data not in the dataset profile.

excluded_borderline_claims.claim_text: LSECs show age-related changes in gene signatures that are closely linked with their function in immune response.
excluded_borderline_claims.reason: Partly interpretive and relies on literature references about LSEC function rather than being directly verifiable from the dataset alone.
